message.content:
You are an independent blind verifier (verifier A) for a production-hardening pass on the hc-assay repo at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. Determine whether this finding is a TRUE_POSITIVE (a genuine production-readiness defect that should be fixed) or a FALSE_POSITIVE (false, already-mitigated, out-of-scope by design, or not a real defect). Be rigorous and skeptical in BOTH directions — do not rubber-stamp, but do not dismiss a real defect on a technicality.

Out-of-scope-by-design exclusions (these are NOT defects): the dataset-agnostic engine receiving opaque inputs; optional import-guarded backends; transcripts intentionally committed (and scrubbed); on-box/loopback data-sovereignty; documented honest-scope limitations.

FINDING K-OPS-2 (medium): regenerate_lockfiles.sh omits requirements-build.lock which CI gates
Location: scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh (+ .github/workflows/ci.yml build-lock sync step + dependabot.yml note)
Claim: CI fails with "requirements-build.lock is stale" by diffing it against a fresh `uv pip compile <(printf "hatchling\neditables\n") ...`. The single documented remediation (scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh, referenced by dependabot.yml and CI error text) does NOT regenerate requirements-build.lock — grep -c returns 0. A maintainer following the documented workflow regenerates the other two locks and stays red, with no scripted path to fix the build lock (must hand-reconstruct the CI command). build-lock pins trove-classifiers (frequent releases).

Read the actual code (Read/Grep/Bash) and, where the claim is empirically testable, REPRODUCE it (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python ... ; use "python -m pytest" not bare pytest). Judge ONLY K-OPS-2. Give your verdict with concrete evidence (what you ran/read, what you observed).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify finding K-OPS-2 by examining the relevant files and reproducing the claim.

content.content:
1	#!/usr/bin/env bash
2	# Regenerate the hash-pinned lockfiles from pyproject.toml (#G-016).
3	#
4	# Dependabot bumps the version ranges in pyproject.toml but cannot regenerate the uv lockfiles,
5	# so a Dependabot PR will fail the CI "requirements.lock is stale" gate until the lockfiles are
6	# regenerated. Run this on a Dependabot branch (or any PR that changes pyproject dependencies),
7	# then commit the updated requirements*.lock files:
8	#
9	#     scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh && git commit -am "chore: regenerate lockfiles"
10	#
11	# uv is pinned to the same version CI uses (#F-031) so the output matches the sync check exactly.
12	set -euo pipefail
13	
14	UV_VERSION="0.11.21"
15	
16	if ! command -v uv >/dev/null 2>&1 || [ "$(uv --version | awk '{print $2}')" != "$UV_VERSION" ]; then
17	  echo "installing uv==$UV_VERSION (CI-pinned) ..." >&2
18	  python -m pip install "uv==$UV_VERSION" >&2
19	fi
20	
21	uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements.lock
22	uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements-core.lock
23	
24	echo "regenerated requirements.lock + requirements-core.lock — review and commit them."
25

content.text: The claim is confirmed by the source. Let me see the full CI sync step context.

content.content:
130	          # that fail this gate on every PR even when the lockfiles are current.
131	          python -m pip install uv==0.11.21
132	          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements.regen.lock
133	          # ignore the auto-generated header (timestamp/command line) when diffing
134	          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements.regen.lock) \
135	            || { echo "::error::requirements.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements.lock"; exit 1; }
136	          # the core lockfile (dev extra only) backs the hash-pinned core lane (#F-004) and must
137	          # stay in sync too.
138	          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock
139	          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements-core.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock) \
140	            || { echo "::error::requirements-core.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements-core.lock"; exit 1; }
141	          # the build-backend lockfile (hatchling etc.) backs the hash-pinned --no-build-isolation
142	          # editable install (#J-003) and must stay in sync.
143	          printf 'hatchling\neditables\n' > /tmp/build-reqs.in
144	          uv pip compile /tmp/build-reqs.in --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements-build.regen.lock
145	          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements-build.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements-build.regen.lock) \
146	            || { echo "::error::requirements-build.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile <(printf 'hatchling\\neditables\\n') --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements-build.lock"; exit 1; }
147	      - name: pip-audit (reproducible known-vulnerability scan)
148	        run: |
149	          set -euo pipefail
150	          # --strict: any dependency that cannot be collected/audited FAILS the gate (no silent
151	          # skips). --require-hashes: every pin must carry a hash. Build --ignore-vuln args as an
152	          # array so allowlist entries can't word-split or glob-expand, and read the file
153	          # newline-agnostically so the last line is honored even without a trailing newline.
154	          IGNORES=()

content.text: The claim is fully confirmed. Let me verify the trove-classifiers point and that the script truly diverges from what CI checks.

input.description: Check build lock contents for trove-classifiers

content.content:
3:editables==0.6 \
7:hatchling==1.30.1 \
14:    # via hatchling
18:    # via hatchling
22:    # via hatchling
23:trove-classifiers==2026.6.1.19 \
26:    # via hatchling

content.text:
All elements of the finding are verified:

1. **CI gates `requirements-build.lock`** — ci.yml lines 143-146 generate a fresh build lock from `printf 'hatchling\neditables\n'` and `diff` it, failing with `::error::requirements-build.lock is stale` on any mismatch.

2. **The documented remediation script omits it** — `scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh` regenerates only `requirements.lock` (line 21) and `requirements-core.lock` (line 22). `grep -c "requirements-build.lock"` returns 0. Its echo on line 24 even states "regenerated requirements.lock + requirements-core.lock" — confirming the build lock is not handled.

3. **The script is the single documented remediation** — `.github/dependabot.yml` line 9 directs maintainers to run `scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh` to make a Dependabot PR mergeable. A maintainer following this regenerates only 2 of the 3 CI-gated locks and stays red, with no scripted path for the third (must hand-reconstruct the `uv pip compile <(...)` command from the CI error text).

4. **trove-classifiers churn is real** — `requirements-build.lock` line 23 pins `trove-classifiers==2026.6.1.19` (a date-versioned, frequently-released transitive of hatchling), so the build lock will genuinely drift and trip this gate.

This is a genuine ops/maintainability defect: the documented one-command remediation is incomplete relative to what CI enforces, causing a broken maintainer workflow. It is in-scope (operational tooling, not an excluded category) and concretely fixable (add the build-lock compile to the script and update the echo).

input.reasoning:
Verified all claims by reading scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh, .github/workflows/ci.yml, .github/dependabot.yml, and requirements-build.lock.

CI gates three lockfiles in the sync step: requirements.lock (ci.yml:132-135), requirements-core.lock (ci.yml:138-140), and requirements-build.lock (ci.yml:143-146). The build-lock check generates a fresh lock via `printf 'hatchling\neditables\n' | uv pip compile ... --generate-hashes` and `diff`s it, failing with `::error::requirements-build.lock is stale` on any drift.

The documented remediation script regenerates only requirements.lock (line 21) and requirements-core.lock (line 22). `grep -c requirements-build.lock scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh` returns 0, confirming omission; its closing echo (line 24) explicitly says only "requirements.lock + requirements-core.lock". 

.github/dependabot.yml:9 names exactly this script as the way to make a Dependabot PR mergeable, so a maintainer following the documented workflow regenerates 2 of 3 CI-gated locks and stays red, with no scripted path for the third (must hand-reconstruct the CI command from the error text).

The churn risk is real: requirements-build.lock:23 pins `trove-classifiers==2026.6.1.19`, a date-versioned, frequently-released transitive of hatchling that will drift and trip the gate.

This is a genuine, in-scope operational/maintainability defect (broken documented remediation workflow), not one of the out-of-scope exclusions, and is concretely fixable by adding the build-lock compile to the script and updating its echo message.

content.text:
Verdict submitted: TRUE_POSITIVE for K-OPS-2.

The finding is a genuine operational defect. `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh` regenerates only `requirements.lock` and `requirements-core.lock` (lines 21-22; grep count for `requirements-build.lock` is 0), while `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (lines 143-146) gates a third lockfile, `requirements-build.lock`, that the documented remediation never touches. `.github/dependabot.yml` (line 9) points maintainers at that script, so following the documented workflow leaves CI red with no scripted fix. The build lock pins the frequently-released `trove-classifiers==2026.6.1.19`, so drift is realistic.
